metadata.title: Longevity & Retirement
metadata.subtitle: Planning for a Longer Financial Future

content.title: Longevity & Retirement
content.subtitle: Planning for a Longer Financial Future

content.label: The Challenge
content.question: How do you plan financially for a retirement that could last 20, 30, or even 40 years?

slides.notes:
DISCUSSION PROMPTS:
THE CHALLENGE (5 min):
â€˘ Present Morgan vs. Jordan scenario. Ask: "Who do you think has more at 65?"
â€˘ Most students will guess Jordan (who invested 4Ă— more). The actual answer surprises them.
â€˘ This counterintuitive result demonstrates why longevity planning starts NOW.

objectives.description: Recognize the importance of planning for retirement early, and how starting young provides enormous advantages through compounding.
Explain key factors in retirement planning: life expectancy, time horizon, inflation, and income needs during retirement years.
Analyze strategies to address potential retirement income shortfalls and manage longevity risk effectively.
Understand how compounding works and develop personal strategies for building retirement savings over time.

terms.definition: How long individuals are expected to live, based on statistical averages, family history, and health factors.
terms.example: A 65-year-old today can expect to live into their mid-80s on average, with many living to 90+

terms.definition: The risk that individuals will live longer than expected and run out of money during retirement.
terms.example: Plan for savings to last 25-30+ years while protecting against outliving your money

terms.definition: The number of years until retirement savings are needed; longer horizons allow for more aggressive investing.
terms.example: A 22-year-old has a 43-year time horizon until age 65 - plenty of time to grow wealth

terms.definition: When retirement savings are insufficient to maintain desired lifestyle; the gap between what you have and what you need.
terms.example: Start early, save consistently, invest wisely, and adjust plans as circumstances change

leftPanel.title: Retirement Is Getting Longer
paragraphs: Life expectancy has increased dramatically over the past century. Today, a 65-year-old can expect to live into their mid-80s, with many living well into their 90s or beyond.
This extended lifespan means retirement savings may need to last 20, 30, or even 40 yearsâ€”far longer than previous generations planned for.

highlightBox.text: í˛ˇ The traditional 10-15 year retirement has evolved into potentially 20-30+ years, requiring substantially more savings.

infoCard.title: Implications of Longer Life:
items: More years to fund with savings
More time for inflation to erode purchasing power
Social Security may not fully support you

leftPanel.title: Compounding: The Snowball Effect
paragraphs: Compounding occurs when your investment returns generate their own returns, creating exponential growth over time.
Morgan invested only $18,000 but ended up with nearly as much as Jordan who invested $70,000. Why? Because Morgan's money had more time to compound.

highlightBox.text: íľŻ Starting 9 years earlier with less money nearly matched investing 4x more but starting later. Time beats money!

layoutData.title: Your Money Loses Value Over Time
paragraphs: Inflation represents one of the most significant challenges to retirement planning. Even modest inflation rates can substantially reduce purchasing power over an extended retirement period.
bulletPoints: At 3% inflation: $100 today = $55 purchasing power in 20 years
In 20 years: What costs $100 today will cost about $181
In 30 years: What costs $100 today will cost about $243
Rule of 72: Prices double approximately every 24 years at 3% inflation

keyPoint.text: đź’° A 25-year retirement means you'll need nearly twice as much income at age 90 as at age 65 just to maintain the same lifestyle!

leftPanel.title: Managing Longevity Risk
paragraphs: One of the greatest risks in retirement is outliving your savings. Several strategies can help address potential income shortfalls:

infoCard.title: Benefits of Delaying Retirement:
items: More years to save and invest
Fewer years to fund in retirement

layoutData.title: The Decisions You Make Today Shape Your Tomorrow
paragraphs: Understanding longevity and retirement planning while you're young gives you the greatest advantage possible: time. The earlier you start, the easier it is to build wealth.
bulletPoints: 40+ years of compounding: Starting at 22 vs 32 can double your retirement savings
Habit formation: Establishing saving habits early makes them automatic
Flexibility: More time allows you to recover from market downturns
Lower required savings: Starting early means smaller monthly contributions
Employer match: Never pass up free money from 401(k) matching
Tax advantages: Roth IRAs provide tax-free growth over decades

slides.notes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS:

1. "I'll only need my retirement savings for 10-15 years."
   â†’ A 65-year-old today has a 25%+ chance of living past 90. Plan for 25-30 years of retirement, not 10-15. Underestimating longevity is one of the biggest retirement planning mistakes.

2. "I can catch up later when I earn more."
   â†’ Math doesn't support this. As Morgan vs. Jordan shows, early dollars are worth FAR more than later dollars due to compounding time. You can't "catch up" on lost timeâ€”you can only start now.

3. "Inflation doesn't matter much for retirement."
   â†’ Over 25 years, 3% inflation doubles prices. What costs $50,000/year at retirement costs $100,000/year 25 years later. Inflation is the "silent thief" that erodes retirement security.

leftPanel.title: Planning for YOUR Longevity
paragraphs: Life expectancy varies based on numerous factors. Understanding these can help you make more accurate projections for your retirement planning.
Consider your personal circumstances across these dimensions when developing your retirement strategy.

highlightBox.text: íżĄ These factors affect both how long you'll live AND your healthcare costs in retirementâ€”plan accordingly.

scenario.name: Keisha's Grandmother: Financial Security at 90
paragraphs: Keisha's grandmother lived to be 90 years oldâ€”significantly longer than the average life expectancy for her generation. Through careful planning and smart investments, she maintained financial security until the end.

scenario.name: Micah's Long-Term Planning at Age 25
paragraphs: Micah, at age 25, has already begun planning for retirement, recognizing that his generation may live even longer than previous ones. He anticipates retiring at 65 and potentially living until 90â€”meaning retirement could last 25 years or more.

content.question:
What specific strategies did Keisha's grandmother and Micah use that you could apply to your own retirement planning?

How does starting in your 20s vs. your 40s change your options?

Why is it important to start thinking about retirement planning even in high school?

How might your career choices and lifestyle habits today affect your financial security 50 years from now?

questions.question: What is longevity risk, and why is it increasingly important for retirement planning?
Why did Morgan end up with almost as much money as Jordan despite investing only $18,000 vs. $70,000?
How does inflation affect the purchasing power of retirement savings over a 30-year retirement?
Name three strategies for addressing potential retirement income shortfalls.

slides.notes:
ANSWER KEY:

1. B (The risk of outliving your savings)

2. Why 22 beats 32: The money invested at 22 has 43 years to compound vs. 33 years for age-32 money. At 7% returns, $1 at age 22 becomes $18.34 at 65; $1 at age 32 becomes only $9.33. Starting 10 years earlier nearly doubles the growth per dollar.

3. Strategies for living 30 years instead of 20: Part-time work in early retirement, delay Social Security claiming to increase benefits, maintain some growth investments throughout retirement, downsize housing, reduce discretionary spending, relocate to lower-cost area. Multiple strategies may be needed.

4. Why start in teens/20s: Compounding requires TIME to work its magic. Starting at 20 gives 45+ years of growth. Starting at 45 gives only 20 years. The early years are worth far more per dollar than later years. Even $50/month at 20 beats $200/month at 40 over the long run.

takeaways.title: Retirement Is Longer
takeaways.description: Today's retirees may need savings to last 20-30+ yearsâ€”far longer than previous generations planned for

takeaways.title: Time Beats Money
takeaways.description: Starting early with small amounts outperforms starting later with larger amountsâ€”compounding is powerful

takeaways.title: Inflation Is Silent
takeaways.description: At 3% inflation, purchasing power is cut in half over 24 yearsâ€”plan for rising costs throughout retirement

takeaways.title: Multiple Strategies Exist
takeaways.description: Delay retirement, increase savings, adjust lifestyle, and invest strategically to manage longevity risk

main.title: íłŠ Life Expectancy & Retirement Analysis
main.description: Analyze life expectancy data across different countries and factors, then consider how this analysis applies to your own retirement planning. Reflect on how healthcare spending, lifestyle choices, and environmental factors might influence both your longevity and retirement needs.

steps: Review the life expectancy data chart
Identify patterns between factors and longevity
Apply insights to your personal situation
Write reflection on how you'd adjust planning
Discuss how lifestyle choices impact retirement
